steps.title: Recognize the vertical shift transformation
steps.content: The equation $y = f(x) + 5$ represents a vertical translation of the original function. Adding 5 to $f(x)$ shifts every point on the graph upward by exactly 5 units. If a point $(x, y)$ lies on the original graph, then $(x, y+5)$ lies on the transformed graph.

steps.title: Identify the horizontal asymptote transformation
steps.content: From the given graph, the original function $f(x)$ approaches $y = 0$ as $x$ increases (the horizontal asymptote is at $y = 0$). When we add 5 to the function, this asymptote shifts upward by 5 units. The new horizontal asymptote is therefore $y = 0 + 5 = 5$.

steps.title: Eliminate choices and verify
steps.content:
Check asymptote location:
  • Choice A: asymptote at $y = -5$ ✗
  • Choice B: asymptote at $y = 0$ ✗
  • Choice C: asymptote at $y = 0$ ✗
  • Choice D: asymptote at $y = 5$ ✓
Verify with a point: At $x = 3$, the original graph shows $f(3) \approx 3$. After the transformation, $(3, 3+5) = (3, 8)$. Choice D lists the point $(3, 8)$, confirming our answer. That matches choice D — D is correct.
